Name: informationtheoreticmixedprecision
Title: Information-Theoretic Framework for Adaptive Mixed-Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By framing mixed-precision quantization as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive an information-theoretic framework that optimally allocates bits to layers based on their task-relevant information content, leading to better compression and performance trade-offs.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored training-free and adaptive mixed-precision quantization methods. However, these methods primarily focus on empirical heuristics and local sensitivity metrics. Our proposal distinguishes itself by deriving a principled information-theoretic framework that quantifies the task-relevant information content of each layer and optimally allocates bits to minimize distortion while maintaining performance.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) is a crucial technique for deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained devices. However, existing methods often rely on empirical heuristics or local sensitivity metrics, which may not capture the global impact of quantization on the model's performance. This paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for adaptive mixed-precision quantization. We frame MPQ as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the distortion in task-relevant information while reducing the bit-width of the model. We derive a principled method to quantify the information content of each layer using mutual information and entropy estimators. Based on this, we formulate an integer linear programming (ILP) problem to optimally allocate bits to layers, ensuring that the most task-critical layers are preserved at higher precision. Our framework is validated on a variety of models and datasets, demonstrating superior performance and compression trade-offs compared to existing methods.
Experiments: - Measure the mutual information between each layer's activations and the task output using InfoNCE-style lower bounds.
- Estimate the entropy of each layer's activations and weights to quantify their information content.
- Compare the estimated information content with the layer's sensitivity to quantization.
- Formulate an ILP problem to allocate bits to layers based on their information content and task-relevant distortion.
- Solve the ILP problem using a standard solver and compare the bit allocation with heuristic methods.
- Evaluate the performance of the quantized model on standard benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-100) and compare with state-of-the-art methods.
- Test the robustness of the proposed framework under different model architectures (e.g., ResNet, ViT) and datasets.
- Evaluate the generalization of the quantized models on unseen data and compare with full-precision models.
- Analyze the trade-offs between compression rate and performance for different bit-width budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: - The mutual information and entropy estimators may have high variance, especially in deep networks with many layers.
- The ILP formulation may be computationally expensive, especially for large models, though it can be optimized using efficient solvers.
- The framework assumes that the task-relevant information can be accurately quantified, which may not always hold in practice.
- The method may not generalize well to all types of models and tasks, requiring further validation and tuning.
